turns.name: JOHN ROBERTS
turns.text: Justice Jackson has the opinion of the court this morning in Case 23-824, United States against Miller.

turns.name: KETANJI BROWN JACKSON
turns.text: This case arises from the collapse of All Resort Group, a Utah-based transportation company that fell into insolvency in 2013 as a result of financial malfeasance and mismanagement. As the company struggled financially, two of its shareholders began misappropriating company funds for their own personal use. At one point, the pair transferred more than $100,000 from the company's accounts to the IRS in order to pay off their own personal tax debts. When the company finally declared bankruptcy three years later, the bankruptcy trustee sought to claw back those federal tax payments so that the money could be used to satisfy the company's debts. To do that, the trustee filed this lawsuit against the federal government seeking to set aside the tax payments or to put it in bankruptcy parlance to avoid the transfers. The Bankruptcy Code gives bankruptcy trustees broad powers to set aside or avoid a debtor's pre-bankruptcy transfer of assets in order to recover those assets for the benefit of the bankruptcy estate. In this case, the trustee relied on the specific avoidance powers contained in §544(b) of the Code. That provision allows a trustee to avoid transfers that would be "voidable under applicable law by an unsecured creditor." In essence, the provision grants the trustee the power to avoid a transfer of a debtor's assets if a creditor would have been able to invalidate the same transfer under some source of law outside of the Bankruptcy Code. Here, the trustee invoked Utah law as the basis for his §544(b) avoidance claim against the government. To bring this suit against the government, the trustee had to rely upon a sovereign immunity waiver, because ordinarily, the government's sovereign immunity would bar any suit against it. The trustee invoked §106(a) of the Bankruptcy Code, an express waiver that abrogates the government's sovereign immunity with respect to various Code provisions including §544. This case requires us to determine the scope of that sovereign immunity waiver. Specifically, we must decide whether §106(a) abrogates sovereign immunity only with respect to the federal cause of action created by §544(b) or whether it also abrogates sovereign immunity with respect to the underlying state law claims that supply the applicable law for that federal cause of action. In an opinion we file with the clerk today, the court holds that §106(a)'s sovereign immunity waiver applies only to the §544(b) claim itself and not to any state law claims nested within that federal claim. Like other waivers of sovereign immunity, §106(a) is properly understood as a jurisdictional provision that empowers courts to hear §544(b) claims against the government to the same extent such claims are viable under state law. But the waiver does not alter the substantive meaning of §544(b)'s applicable law clause by allowing a trustee to set aside a transfer that would not be voidable by a creditor outside of bankruptcy proceedings. That understanding of the waiver's scope vindicates the text of §106(a) itself which explicitly states that "nothing in this section shall create any substantive claim for relief or cause of action not otherwise existing under this title, the Federal Rules of Bankruptcy Procedure, or non-bankruptcy law." Our reading of §106(a) also preserves the long-settled understanding of §544(b) as bestowing the same rights on a trustee that an actual creditor would enjoy outside of bankruptcy. And our reading of §106(a) further comports with the general rule that waivers of sovereign immunity are to be construed narrowly. Respondent contends that §106(a)'s use of the phrase with respect to necessitates a broad reading of the statute's sovereign immunity waiver and he further contends that the waiver would be rendered meaningless by permitting the government to invoke sovereign immunity as a basis for arguing that the transfer is not voidable by an actual creditor under state law. Neither of these arguments is availing. The phrase "with respect to" is inherently context dependent and as just explained, the context here cuts decidedly against respondent's broad statutory reading, nor does our reading of the statute render the waiver meaningless. To the contrary, our reading preserves the waiver's intended effect with respect to §544(b) suits against state governments and with respect to suits under §544's other sub-provision, subsection (a). For all of these reasons and others we lay out in our written opinion, the judgment of the Tenth Circuit is reversed. Justice Gorsuch has filed a dissenting opinion.
